rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: GBVPR - Gender-based Violence Prevention and Response Project
rows.description: With its longstanding experience in promoting psychosocial support services and a strong engagement in support of federal state building, Switzerland is well positioned to engage on reducing all forms of GBV and discrimination against women in Nepal. The project aims at strengthening and institutionalizing a coordinated, survivor-centred response for GBV victims, addressing root causes and engaging with all government levels for a more gender equitable policy environment.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Improving sexual and reproductive health and rights and biodiversity conservation through integrated approach in Nepal
rows.description:
This project focuses on improving sexual and reproductive health and rights and biodiversity conservation in Nepal. This project forms a part of a larger population health and environment (PHE) project which is implemented jointly by Väestöliitto Family 
Planning Association of Nepal FPAN WWF Finland and WWF Nepal and continues previous cooperation (2011-2014) between these partners. A model of integrated approach is applied in the implementation of the project. The project will be implemented during 2015-
2017 in eight districts of Nepal?s Terai region. People in these districts have low access to quality sexual and reproductive information and services which leads to unwanted pregnancies and sexually transmitted infections. Widespread poverty and populatio
n growth in Terai region also cause unsustainable use of natural resources. Aim of the project is empowerement increase of information capacity and well-being as well as improved livelihood of young people and natural resource management communities. This 
project also aims at strengthening partneships. Project will implement trainings strengthen youth networks and resource centers improve the quality and amount of sexual health services and offer vocational training combined with micro credits. Partnerships
 and networks will be strengthened to disseminate information about integrated approach.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Unlocking doors to early childhood interventions for health and education for children with vision or hearing impairment in Nepal
rows.description:
This project aims to increase the enrollment of disabled children at the pre-school age of 3-6 years in public schools simultaneously with other children of the age group. Identification of children with disabilities or disabling diseases is conducted by e
xamining screened children at the eye + ear camps. Around 10 000 children will be provided with rehabilitation and/or simple treatment necessary for school attendance and prevention of additional morbidity. An estimated 75 children with severe visual or he
aring disability will undergo a four month rehabilitation in the hospital run by the local partner organization (Children?s Hospital for Eye Ear and Rehabilitation Services CHEERS). To reach these goals volunteers are trained among female health workers te
achers and students so they can identify children with neurosensory disabilities or at risk of disability whom will be sent to eye + ear camps for medical evaluation. This contributes to reducing the prevalence of disabling neurosensory conditions which is
 also achieved by raising awareness of health related to sight and hearing among locals as well as decisionmakers. The project envisages all four aspects of health care delivery; preventive promotive curative and rehabilitative services. The project is to 
be realized as a three-year pilot in two districts in the Terai region of Nepal. The project is to be realized as a collaboration between a Nepalese NGO called B.P. Eye Foundation which has developed the project idea and PSR Finland which has been contacte
d by B.P. Eye foundation to act as a project partner. Based on the previous work of B.P.Eye Foundation this collaborative project also aims to change the Nepalese view on the rights of the disabled remove barriers and stigmas associated with disabilities a
nd enable children with disabilities to become productive citizens. For sustainability of the activities the government of Nepal has made a preliminary commitment of implementing these protocols in other districts and eventually the whole of Nepal once the
 results of the pilot project turn out to be encouraging. B.P. Eye Foundation holds monthly meetings with the government and representatives from Early Childhood Development Center. The project is planned to start in 2015 with project duration of three yea
rs.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Preventive Education for Uterine Prolapse in Nepal
rows.description:
In Nepal there are about 14 million women with Uterine prolapse. Though the expected lifetime has become longer in Nepal the amount of women who get uterine prolapse has not decreased. It has doubled from the year 2008. The problem is in the Nepalese cultu
re. It does not protect the the women after they given a birth to a child. Many of the women are expected to work hard soon after the delivery.The aim of this project is to secure a better health for all in Nepal and to prevent Uterine prolapse- by support
ing them to appreciate the motherhood and to protect the mothers after the delivery - by counselling in breast-feeding and in child care- by teaching right ways to lift and to work - by guiding to healthy living habits and versatile diet- by encouraging to
 smoke free lifestyle with no intoxicating agents or fear of AIDSIt is planned that the project will reach all the 75 districts in Nepal in three years. Partners in the districts will be the health and education departments of the districts and also the vo
luntary women workers groups which already exist in the districts.  According to the plan in each district a three day seminar will be arranged in which all the health teachers from the schools health assistants midwives and the voluntary women workers in 
the district will be invited. Each year about 2500 health workers can take part in the seminar (100 in each district).  All these will get the health education teaching material used during the seminar so that they can use it also in their own work. So the
 amount of the Nepalese reached by the project will be several time larger.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: UN Women - Women´s Economic Empowerment
rows.description:
UN Women´s Women´s Economic Empowerment project aims to enhance the economic situation of especially the most poorest groups of Nepalese women. The participation of Nepalese women in the advancement of the national economy is limited due to insufficient 
economic resources and low level of education. The project  advances the consciousness of the beneficiaries regarding their rights through networks of marginalized women. The project builds up the capacity of the Government of Nepal to follow the condition
s of equality in its economic policy both at national and provincial level. Specific beneficiaries can be named female migrant workers domestic servants HIV positive women victims of conflicts and human trafficking and the female farmers of remote areas. U
N Women´s office in Nepal will execute the project and contract will be concluded with the main office of UN Women. Finland will support the project with 4 000 000 euros from 2015 to 2017.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Support to Partnerships for Community Development
rows.description: This project aims to help strengthen communities in under-served areas by improving education, vocational training and health care, and by developing local businesses. Project activities include: (1) provide equipment and training to improve schools and teaching; (2) increase access to quality healthcare and services, and develop local businesses and community networks; (3) provide better instructions and learning material for teachers, trainers, schools and students, especially women and girls; (4) provide local health providers and community members with improved equipment and health service delivery; and (5) provide training to local businesses including women entrepreneurs and farmers with improved business skills and micro-finance.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Maternal Child Health Initiative - World Neighbours Canada
rows.description: The initiative aims to address several development needs and challenges all linked to underlying poverty and lack of basic services in the remote rural areas in the Ramechap district in Nepal, in the Azabache and Nacaome areas in Honduras and in the Gourma province in Burkina Faso. The overall objective of the initiative is to improve the health of mothers and young children, and thus contribute to reducing maternal and child mortality, through assistance with village and household-scale infrastructure, parent health education and enhanced nutrition and food security. World Neighbours Canada aims to develop and implement training programs for women and men to learn about family planning options, to increase food crop production using sustainable and ecologically sound practices, to promote proper nutrition in infants and young children under two years of age that includes breast feeding promotion, developing and implementing water system plans with participation of users etc. The project proposes to benefit more than 50,000 women, men, girls and boys.  World Neigbours Canada is working with Tamakoshi Sewa Samiti (TSS) in Nepal, L’Association d’Appui à la Promotion du Développement Durable des Communautés (APDC) in Burkina Faso, and Vecinos Honduras, in Honduras to implement this project.  This project is part of Canada’s commitment to maternal, newborn and child health programming.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Strengthening Health Systems and Improving Nutrition in Nepal and Vietnam
rows.description: This project works in collaboration with local governments in Nepal and Vietnam to make necessary health services and interventions accessible to children less than two years of age and their mothers. Research has identified that the first 1,000 days of a child’s life are critical for intellectual and physical development and lifelong health. Nepal and Vietnam have both made significant strides in reducing infant and maternal mortality and improving access to essential health care. However, inequality in terms of mortality and access to services between different regions and ethnic groups remains a challenge in both of these countries. This project addresses this inequality by working to improve the health care systems of remote regions (Banke district in Nepal) and ethnic minorities in the two countries (the Thai, H’Mong, Xinh Mun, Kho Mu and Khang of Vietnam).  Project activities include: (1) improving health management capacity; (2) improving delivery of and access to quality health care and nutrition services; (3) promoting care-seeking; (4) improving nutrition; (5) engaging men and family members to reduce gender-related barriers; (6) providing capacity building to health management teams and health workers directly; (7) engaging and offering education to men and key family members on the importance of maternal, newborn and child health (MNCH) and how they can support the health of women and children; and (8) increasing the awareness of Canadians in MNCH issues through public engagement initiatives.  The project reach is a total of 38,418 direct beneficiaries in Nepal (18,056 women, 16,671 men and 3,691 children) and 20,611 in Vietnam (10,001 women, 9,172 men and 1,438 children). An additional 9,200 Canadians are reached through a public engagement component.  This project is implemented in collaboration with the following HealthBridge Foundation of Canada local partners: The International Nepal Fellowship (INF) in Nepal who is implementing the project in the Banke district, located in the Mid-Western region and the Centre for Creative Initiatives for Health and Population (CCIHP) in Lai Chau province in the remote, mountainous North-West region of Vietnam. Both the Banke district and Lai Chau province are among the poorest areas in the two countries with high mortality rates and the least access to essential health services.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Nepal / WHO / Humanitarian assistance for the victims of Nepal's earthquake
rows.description:
A 7.8 magnitude earthquake struck Nepal on 25 April creating large scale damage and many casualties. According to initial estimations and based on the latest earthquake intensity mapping over 8 million people are affected in 39 of Nepal?s 75 districts. Ove
r 2 million people live in the 11 most critically hit districts. The UN Flash Appeal calls for US$415 million to respond to the most urgent humanitarian needs for the next three months. Finland will allocate 500 000 euros in humanitarian assistance to the 
victims of the earthquake in Nepal through World Health Organisation (WHO).

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Technical Assistance for Competence-Based Soft Skills Development in School Education in Nepal
rows.description:
The Technical Assistance for Competence-Based Soft Skills Development in School Education in Nepal (TASS) is the second phase of the Finnish funded TSSP program which was implemented in the years 2012-2015. The aim of the TSSP program was to improve the qu
ality of education in Nepal by integrating soft skills into teaching. Soft skills are defined as personal cooperation thinking innovation and business skills. The TSSP project ends in December 2015.The mid-term review of TSSP concluded that the integration
 of soft skills into the education sector in Nepal is highly relevant as it can improve both the quality and the relevance of education. The evaluation recommended a continuation of the project. The main achievements of TSSP were the approval of new curric
ula in six different subjects for grades 9-10 as well as the development of teachers training materials and their testing. The new textbooks for grade 9 will be in schools in 2016 and teachers' training packages have been taught in 40% of Nepal's teacher t
raining facilities. The second phase ? the TASS project ? will build on the achievements of the first phase. During the TASS program the update of the curriculum for grades 9-10 moves to the implementation stage. Teaching materials are being developed also
 for grades 6-8 and work on materials for grades 9-10 continues. The education of teachers and capacity building of relevant institutions regarding the application of soft skills also continues.   The beneficiaries of the project are Nepalese students in g
rades 6-10. The immediate beneficiaries are the Ministry of Education and regional and district level education directorates and offices. In addition the projects 80 pilot schools and their teachers benefit from focused capacity building support. The Minis
try of Education in Nepal implements the project. The proposed budget for the project is 17 MEUR for the years 2016-2018.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: UNICEF - WASH Earthquake Recovery Programme
rows.description:
The catastrophic earthquake of 25th April 2015 measuring 7.8 Richter scale had its devastating consequences on human lives and property as well as infrastructures including WASH services in Nepal. The gains that Nepal had made so far in achieving the MDG t
argets on water and sanitation has been negatively challenged by the damages of water supply and sanitation services. UNICEF WASH Earthquake Recovery Programme will support reconstruction efforts in Nepal particularly in WASH sector with a particular focus
 on Disaster Risk Management. The expected results and outcomes of the programme are formulation and operationalization of disaster risk reduction and climate change policies capacity building and system building for disaster preparedness and response and 
earthquake affected water supply schemes and school toilets and water systems rehabilitation. Rehabilitated water supply schemes will serve at least 33 000 people and school toilets and water systems rebuilding will serve at least 20 000 children. At least
 20 000 school children will be also educated on Hand washing.Finland will support the programme with 1 million Euros in 2016.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Support to Nepal's School Sector Development Plan (SSDP)
rows.description:
The School Sector Development Plan (SSDP) program is the second phase of the first education sector program in Nepal the SSRP (School Sector Reform Program) which Finland supports over the years 2009-2016. The most important achievements of the SSRP were t
he substantial increase in access to education achievement of gender parity in education a decrease in illiteracy and classroom size and an increase in the number of educated teachers. The second phase of the education sector program the SSDP will begin in
 July 2016. The SSDP is based on the achievements of the first stage and in order to ensure continuity also the main objectives remain largely the same. In the SSDP there is however a much stronger focus on enhancing the quality equitable participation and
 learning outcomes in education. There is also a stronger emphasis on tying disbursements of funds to achieved targets. The objectives of the program are divided into four categories: equity quality efficiency and relevance. The program aims to enhance the
se aspects of education on all levels: early childhood education basic education secondary education and lifelong learning. The program strives to ensure that all students in Nepal have equal opportunities to access high quality education.  A reform of the
 governance and management system of the education sector is also part of the objectives of the program. The final beneficiaries of the program are Nepal's around 7.3 million students and almost 29.000 public schools. The Ministry of Education of Nepal is 
implementing the program and the Government of Nepal funds at least 80% of its budget. Other development partners who fund the program include the Asian Development Bank Australia the EU the World Bank Norway UNICEF and Global Partnership for Education. Fi
nland's contribution to the program is proposed to be 20 MEUR over the years 2016-2020.

Switzerland’s convening power to bring together federal, provincial and local governments will be catalytic in promoting intergovernmental cooperation in view of strengthening local economic development.

rows.description: The proposed interventions support the Government of Nepal in its efforts to contain the further spread of COVID-19 and to address the socio-economic consequences, in particular for disadvantaged groups. As a trusted partner and with its longstanding experience in development cooperation, Switzerland is well positioned to effectively provide relief and recovery during, and in the aftermath of the pandemic, in close collaboration with local and international stakeholders.

It will work with the Nepali Government to provide reintegration services and support policy development to anchor them in the federal structure. Switzerland has a key interest that the potential of migration for development is fully used and is well positioned to engage on the topic, given its track record in migration in Nepal.

rows.description:
The project is about gender equality. Cricket Association of the Blind CAB wants to raise awareness of gender equality to stop the harassment of blind women cricket players, and disabled women in general. CAB will organize two sexual awareness conferences 
and a mixed sexes cricket game.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Empowering women with disability through skill development training and employment opportunity
rows.description: Twenty women with disabilities will be taught handicrafts for six months. The products will be showcased by establishing a small shop in Thamel area in Kathmandu. The shop will be rented for six months. Thamel is a well known market area.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Creating Space to Take Action on Violence Against Women and Girls
rows.description: This program aims to reduce violence against women and girls, and the prevalence of child, early and forced marriage (CEFM) through coordinated prevention and response activities in Bangladesh, Indonesia, Nepal, Pakistan, and Philippines. Project activities include: 1) creating an enabling environment to prevent violence against women and girls by engaging key community leaders (including religious, private sector and political) and youth in advancing women’s leadership and women’s rights; 2) improving access to economic opportunities and to services such as shelters, and legal and psycho-social support; 3) reinforcing the use of innovative knowledge, including best and emerging practices, and accountability systems to end violence against women and girls, and CEFM. This project reaches 245,000 direct and 980,000 indirect beneficiaries (mainly women and girls).

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Sustainable Use of Technology for Public Sector Accountability
rows.description: This initiative seeks to close existing gaps between demand from non-governmental organizations, communities and citizens for services, and supply by governments of services through the use of integrated technologies. This project focuses on women and marginalized groups to increase their access to and skills in using technology and supports them in developing the leadership capacities necessary to use integrated technologies effectively to voice their concerns collectively and to influence decision-making within government structures at the local and national levels. Project activities include: (1) capacity building through training, coaching and technical support; (2) developing and/or adapting specific technological mechanisms and tools (for example mobile applications); (3) establishing infrastructure technology facilities, namely techno hubs, at the national, district and local levels to facilitate information sharing; and (4) implementing an awareness campaign on the importance of civic engagement and participatory monitoring of public service delivery.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Reconstruction of Water, Sanitation and Hygiene Services
rows.description: On April 25, 2015, a 7.8-magnitude earthquake struck Nepal and was followed by a series of large aftershocks. The earthquakes resulted in over 8,700 deaths and 22,000 injuries, 2.8 million displaced and 3.5 million in need of food assistance. In response to the earthquake, Canada established the Nepal Earthquake Relief Fund that committed the Government of Canada to matching eligible private donations to approved Canadian organizations.  With funding from the Nepal Earthquake Relief Fund, the project improves the wellbeing of 260,000 people by increasing access to and use of safe drinking water, sanitation and hygiene (WASH) services in severely and crisis-hit earthquake affected areas in Nepal. The project trains local government officials and community organizations to respond to the current and future WASH needs of these communities, building resilience for the long-term. Beneficiaries are people unserved or under-served by adequate WASH services and include women, children and people of lower castes. Local governments and community organizations educate over 250,000 people, at least half of whom are women, on how to use WASH services and practice healthy hygiene behaviours. The project advances women's participation as decision-makers on critical WASH and health issues, and ultimately reduces gender inequalities in access to and control over WASH-related resources and benefits of development. The project focuses on the districts of Dolakha, Sindhupalchowk, Nuwakot and Kavrepalanchok. Activities are implemented by a national Water Expertise and Training Centre, hosted by the Environment and Public Health Organization.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake Relief Fund Projects - Monitoring and Evaluation
rows.description: This project represents funds set aside for monitoring and evaluation of the Nepal Earthquake Relief Fund initiatives (5 projects), as well as for administrative purposes, contingencies, and technical assistance. For administrative reasons, sometimes the funds identified for this purpose are included in the main project budget and sometimes, as in this case, they are assigned a separate project number.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Establishing the Rights of Marginalized Indigenous Tamang Children through Culturally Sensitive Learning
rows.description:
There are several ethnic groups and indigenous people in Nepal, and the country has internationally committed to improve the rights of indigenous people. The tamang language is part of Tibetan Burman languages, and it’s among the five most spoken languag
es in Nepal. Implementing linguistic rights has been a huge challenge for Nepal, in which over 100 languages are spoken and in which structural reforms are still ongoing. Even though time has been reserved for education of both Nepalese and a local languag
e, English is often taught instead of a local language due to lack of linguistic knowledge and negative attitudes. The project continues directly from a project previously funded by KIOS. The aim is to promote right to education on one’s mother tongue am
ongst children speaking Tamang language. Also the continuation of the project seeks to enhance tamang-speaking children’s cultural and educational rights in schools as well as inform about linguistic and cultural rights to the larger public. Childspace F
oundation Nepal was founded in 2003 by democracy and human rights activists belonging in tamang linguistic minority and indigenous population. The organization was originally registered as Helping Society Nepal in 2003, but the name was changed into Childs
pace Foundation Nepla in 2011. The organisation’s activities focus especially on improving the status and rights of tamang minority and, on a larger scale, on enhancing the rights of indigenous people and ethnic minorities. www.childspacefoundation.org

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Nepal Development Programme
rows.description:
Marginalised people are not prepared for, able to respond and timely recover from the impact of disaster. Poverty, injustice, inequality and discrimination persist in the communities. Therefore, there is a need to implement the project in Kailali, Dailekh,
 Doti, Lalitpur, Morang and Jhapa districts of Nepal.Nepal Development Programme aims at improving food insecurity, sustainable livelihoods and gender equality as well as decease caste/ethnicity-based discriminations, mitigate disaster and climate change i
mpacts and empower towards enforcement of human rights as well as participation in decision making at local and national context. Programme covers 30 Village Development Committees (VDCs). The implementing partner (LWF Nepal) intends to work with 3,000 fam
ilies who are poor, most vulnerable and disadvantaged. Activities include Disaster risk management /climate change risk management, community empowerment and awareness raising, infrastructure and agriculture development, women empowerment, advocacy and net
working, improving access and facilities to education , higher / vocational education support , civic education, human rights monitoring, strengthening community based organisation (CBOs) and their networks and capacity development for peace and developmen
t. Project is implemented by Finn Church Aid’s local partner Lutheran World Federation (LWF) Nepal.Rights-holders /beneficiaries: Marginalised, vulnerable and conflict-affected people such as Dalit, freed bonded labourers, disaster affected population, e
thnic minorities, landless, women-headed households and childrenImplementing partner:    The Lutheran World Federation Nepal (LWF Nepal).

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Actions for Advancing Freedom of Freed Haliyas, Kamaiyas and Kamlaharis
rows.description:
Freed Haliyas, ex-Kamaiyas and Kamlaris (HaKK) have not yet fully realised the true sense of freedom. They are aware but still lacking desired level of empowerment. Thousands of right-holders are still denied their rights and entitlements. Therefore, right
-holders need to be further supported to empower them to claim and enjoy their rights. The duty-bearers require enough support in formulation policies, strategies, guidelines and implement those aiming the just rehabilitation of freed HaKK. The project, th
us, focuses on livelihood, education, capacity building and advocacy to facilitate duty-bearers’ and right-holders’ efforts to end this bondage system permanently. The overall goal of the project is to enhance the dignified living of freed Haliyas, Ex-
 Kamaiyas and Kamlaris. The project aims at advancing socio-economic condition of freed Haliya, ex- Kamaiyas and Kamlaris enhancing their access to livelihood assets and promoting equality and non-discrimination Activities: • Empowerment education and so
cial animation• Organisational development • Establish functional rehabilitation fund • Evidence based advocacy and campaign at local, national level• Train and mobilise activists and human rights defenders • Support duty-bearers to implement Hal
iya / Kamaiya rehabilitation plan and Kamlari education guideline• Promotion of small-holder agriculture and farm / off—farm based micro and / or small scale enterprises• Increasing access of communities in livelihood assets and markets• Establish 
and strengthen women–led and women-managed cooperatives• Support students to continue their secondary and higher secondary education• School facility improvement, teaching learning material support• Provide skill development training aiming at empl
oymentRights-holders /beneficiaries: Freed Haliyas, ex- Kamaiyas and Kamlaris.Implementing partner:  The Lutheran World Federation Nepal (LWF Nepal)

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Equality and Non-Discrimination for Sustainable Peace (ENDS)
rows.description:
The project will intervene to raise awareness among both Dalits and non-Dalits and prepare them to speak up against unlawful and inhuman practices of caste-based discrimination and untouchability, educate and sensitise government authorities on the anti-di
scrimination legal provisions and measures. The project will mobilise human rights defenders for supporting victims and empower them to seek justice through formal process, and educate and empower women and youths to engage in peace building process in the
ir community. This will contribute for the protection and promotion of fundamental human rights and freedom of Dalit, women and other marginalised groups in Nepal.Activities: • Monitoring / tracking caste based discrimination and human right violation ca
ses mobilising human rights defenders• Documentation of human rights violation cases and dissemination• Evidence based advocacy• Raising awareness on the justice procedures and legal provisions related to non-discriminations and equality• Provide l
egal support to the victims• Documentation of good initiatives of religious leaders and their associations for promoting non-discrimination and equality• Vertical and horizontal dialogues among government and non-government stakeholders combating caste
 based discriminations and promoting social harmony minimising tensions.• Marking relevant national / International days• Mobilising and connecting NNDSWO’s VDC level committee members in human rights defenders networks• Fact finding/case investiga
tion and filing of First Information RegistrationRights-holders /beneficiaries:  Dalit women and youth Implementing partner:  Nepal National Dalit Social Welfare Organization (NNDSWO)

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Addressing Concerns of Nepalese Dalit through Second Cycle of UPR
rows.description:
Addressing Concerns of Nepalese Dalit through Second Cycle of UPR. The project is to promote and protect human rights of Dalit in Nepal. The Specific Objective is to contribute for addressing the key human rights concerns of Dalit through second cycle of U
niversal Periodic Review (UPR). The specific human rights issues and concerns of Dalit community discussed and documented through a participatory process with the involvement of more than 200 Dalit organizations representing all five development regions of
 Nepal. A report on the specific human rights issues and concerns of Dalit will be developed and submitted to UPR concerned committee of UN within third week of March 2015.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Grassroots Dialogues and Sustainable rehabilitation and reconstruction
rows.description:
Program I 1. Grassroots and National level dialogues will be organised under the Social Forum banner. SADED, through its grassroots dialogue, collects memories, thoughts and experience from the grassroots level 2. Those interesting and important memories a
nd outcomes of the dialogues will be published and disseminated through newsletters, newspaper articles, blogs and websites.  3. SADED-Nepal prioritizes to interact with various social movements in South Asia. Since it is easy for Nepalese to visit Pakista
n, and Bangladesh in comparison to Indians, SADED-Nepal will engage with social movements there so that regional social movements  especially Social Forum will be strengthened. Program II: Sustainable rehabilitation and reconstruction (after 2015 earthquak
e)  1. To stay with our green principles to use local technological skills and material for reconstruction: There is a risk that some of the multinational companies will monopolize the reconstruction discourse in order to expand their capital. SADED-Nepal 
has a plan to recruit some of the technicians to mobilize in the affected areas before and during the reconstruction work. They will be given chance to have exposure visits to places like Uttarakhand and Assam of India. These technicians will be able to su
ggest a good engineered structures so that the reconstructed houses with local materials will be equally or even more stronger than that of corporate promoted "modern" houses. Dialogues will be organized in the affected areas with the participation of comm
unity-based organizations and local elites. Also we will invite experts from Uttarakhand and Assam for sharing dialogues in the affected areas.  2. Resuming/Building/supporting public spaces for children and elders in the villages: SADED-Nepal have identif
ied 5 Village Development Communities (VDCs) in Nuwakot  and Rasuwa/Sindupalchowk from where we start relief and reconstruction work of children's space. While doing so, pre-primary schools, play schools along with the village based public spaces will be r
econstructed where it is destroyed. We will take a VDC, and after having need assessments in the villages, will help reconstruct damaged infrastructures so that public spaces can resume/start their activities.  Elderly and young people will be involved in 
running these children spaces. Furthermore, midday meals and play materials will be provided in a school and a children’s play space.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Reintegration of Returnee Migrant Workers Project
rows.description: The project will contribute to ensure migrant workers benefit fully from their migration experience by supporting the reintegration of returnee migrant workers in Nepal. It will work with the Nepali Government to provide reintegration services and support policy development to anchor them in the federal structure. Switzerland has a key interest that the potential of migration for development is fully used and is well positioned to engage on the topic, given its track record in migration in Nepal.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Udaya – Investment and Innovation for Economic Development
rows.description: This programme aims for women and men to benefit from socioeconomic progress through growth and innovation of small and medium enterprises (SMEs) in Province 1 of Nepal. Working with the provincial government, it will enhance the framework conditions for SMEs by enabling access to pertinent business development services, growth capital, and information on relevant public initiatives and services. It leverages Switzerland’s expertise in private sector development, blended finance, and federalisation.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Quality TVET for Youth (QualiTY)
rows.description: The overall goal of the project is for Nepalese youth to benefit from quality Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) and better employment prospects. To achieve this, the project will support the federal, provincial and local governments of Nepal to establish an accreditation and coaching system so that TVET schools can improve and meet national quality requirements. The project capitalises on Switzerland’s long-standing and unique expertise in TVET federalisation.

rows.title: WHO coordinates and leads the health cluster or sector and partners to assess health needs and develop, fund and monitor humanitarian health emergency response plans in humanitarian emergencies
rows.description: Pandemic Influenza Preparedness Programme

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Skills and Reconstruction Project in Western Nepal (SRP)
rows.description: An earthquake of magnitude 6.4 struck Western Nepal in November 2023, leaving 154 people dead and 375 injured. It was estimated at the time of writing that over 62,000 houses and 600 schools were damaged. Building on the experience in recovery initiatives after the 2015 earthquake, this project will augment government’s efforts in reconstruction of public infrastructure such as schools and provide vocational training on earthquake-resilient construction to masons in the affected area.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: ReCAP - Regional Climate Action Partnership in the Hindu Kush Himalayas
rows.description: The Hindu Kush Himalayas are highly vulnerable to climate disasters. Multi-hazard risk models and early warning systems are pivotal for de-risking public and private investments and protection of communities. In the first project phase, Switzerland provides technical assistance to the Government of Nepal, local private companies and academia to build required institutional capacities in close collaboration with the Asian Development Bank and the World Bank.

rows.reporting: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO)
rows.title: Living Heritage and The Sustainable Development Goals: Safeguarding intangible cultural heritage through formal and non-formal education in Asia and the Pacific and in Africa
rows.description: In 2022-2023, the project developed and piloted learning tools/pedagogies on the integration of living heritage in non-formal education in the Community Learning Centers in Nepal and Thailand aiming to strengthen the safeguarding and transmission of ICH, improve the relevance of education programmes and promote community well-being social cohesion and SD. In the 2024-2025 biennium, the project is implemented in Benin, where it proposes to bring schools closer to communities and their social and cultural environment, to enhance the generational transmission of living heritage and to improve the academic performance of learners. The project constitutes a pilot in two districts of the country - Aplahoué and Bohicon- that will help assess and consolidate the approach and provide the Government of Benin with tools for its eventual implementation at the national level.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Municipal Revenue Enhancement Project
rows.description: Nepal faces critical challenges in achieving fiscal autonomy and accountability at the local government level, undermining effective governance. Through this project, Switzerland supports Nepal to directly confront these issues by strengthening local governments’ capacity to generate revenue and deliver essential services. By engaging at the local government level, where citizenstate interactions are most tangible, the project will demonstrate the benefits of inclusive federalism.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Nepal Impact Investment Community 2 (NIIC2)
rows.description: SDC's contribution to the Nepal Impact Investment Community Fund 2 will address the gaps in the financial ecosystem and market failures that limit access to capital and constrain growth for Digital Economy Catalyst (DEC) SMEs. By bringing debt and equity together with in-built business development services support, the initiative will enable capital providers to address the needs of DEC SMEs and enable these SMEs to unlock growth, foster innovation, and create impact through the creation of job and income opportunities.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Technological platforms – monitoring
rows.description: This project represents funds set aside for the monitoring and evaluation of 7 technological platforms to strengthen public sector accountability and citizen engagement projects.  For administrative reasons, sometimes the funds identified for this purpose are included in the projects’ budgets and sometimes, as in this case, they are assigned to a separate project number.

rows.title: ED NDL 7 - SDG 4 coordination; UNCT coordination
rows.description: Coordinating activities among S Asian countries in collaboration with UNESCO DHA and KAT

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Arts and Culture Programme in Nepal
rows.description: The Arts and Culture programme aims to invigorate Nepal's arts sector by partnering with three local cultural spaces focused on documentaries, music and photography. it promotes creative expression, professional growth, gender equality and social inclusion and community engagement, contributing to cultural development and an inclusive Nepali society. The programme aligns with SDC’s Thematic Guidance on Culture and Development, supporting independent, diverse and participatory cultural sectors.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Subnational Governance Program (SNGP)
rows.description: Politicians, the three spheres of government, civil society and the citizenry at large need access to relevant information on federalism and opportunities to engage in meaningful dialogue to deepen and sustain the reforms. This project seeks to enhance the capacity of media and academia to generate evidence-based information and foster discourse on key aspects of Nepal’s federalism, and it supports sub-national governments to engage in exchange and enhanced cooperation.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Municipal Revenue Enhancement Project
rows.description: Nepal faces critical challenges in achieving fiscal autonomy and accountability at the local government level, undermining effective governance. Through this project, Switzerland supports Nepal to directly confront these issues by strengthening local governments’ capacity to generate revenue and deliver essential services. By engaging at the local government level, where citizenstate interactions are most tangible, the project will demonstrate the benefits of inclusive federalism.

rows.reporting: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO)
rows.title: Safeguarding Living Heritage for the sustainable development of communities in Nepal
rows.description: The activity aims to protect the Living Heritage in Nepal by strengthening national capacities and providing policy guidance. In collaboration with UNESCO Category II Centres like ICHCAP and CRIHAP, as well as other sectors, the activity will support national capacity-building for safeguarding and promoting Living Heritage to empower communities. These efforts will focus on the role of ICH and its safeguarding in the context of climate change and integrate the safeguarding of living heritage into broader sectoral strategies for achieving sustainable development. Additionally, technical assistance will be provided to support Nepal's participation in regional periodic reporting. Nepal is among 42 countries in Asia and the Pacific undertaking its first regional cycle of reporting under the revised 2018 Overall Results Framework, 21 of which, including Nepal are submitting their reports for the first time.

rows.title: Strengthening Community Engagement for Biodiversity Conservation in KCA - Project ID: 00141045
rows.description: The proposed project will enhance biodiversity documentation and conservation in the Kanchenjunga Conservation Area by introducing scientific tools and integrating indigenous knowledge. rows.reporting: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO)
rows.title: TVET, Literacy, Higher Education, STEM Education
rows.description:
Technical support will be provided for revising the curriculum in TVET sector and further work on detailing Higher Education Qualification Farmwork and disseminating it. Additional technical assistance will be provided for the future landscaping higher education in Nepal. Furthermore, ntegrating Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, and Mathematics (STEAM) into school education
The integration of STEAM education into the curriculum, as prioritized by Nepal’s National Education Policy 2076, is essential for fostering a holistic and interdisciplinary approach to learning that equips students with the necessary skills for the future. Integrating STEAM (Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, and Mathematics) education not only enhances students' engagement and motivation by connecting academic content to real-life contexts but also equips them with the knowledge and skills necessary to address contemporary challenges such as climate change, economic development, and social equity. In this context, UNESCO aims at supporting the government to better understand the needs and priorities in STEAM education, ensuring more effective integration for improved learning outcomes and helping learners become responsible and active citizens.

rows.reporting: Global Green Growth Institute
rows.title: Sanitation and Climate change, assessing resilience and emissions
rows.description: The Universities of Bristol/Leeds/Sydney should be recieving a grant of around 1MUSD from Gates totest and refine a methodology on GHG emissions for Sanitation. The consortium and Gates is looking for us to bring in buy-in at an institutional level and incorporate this methodology in policy work in Nepal, Uganda, Ethiopia and possibly Senegal.The initiative is led by Professor Guy Howard (an ex WHO and DFID lead - pioneer in linking WASH to climate) and sees a role for GGGI especially for buy in at institutional level through our country presence. The aim isnt to levy heavy funding but more to tap into reputed academics and QA on emissions work so as to strengthen our policy work. The policy work in turn can lead to other more GIS work, or as in the case of the current Gates grant and the KOICA project in Uganda strengthen the policy work that has been encouraged/requested. The funding is currently proposed to go through the universities. They are concerned that if it doesn go through them it will be hard to control the deliverables. This can be discussed. There is some synergy with the current Gates grant and so we may be able to pool some of resources from there for Nepal and Senegal as well as tapping into the 1MUSD grant which spans 3yrs. In view of our mainly buy in /policy work it may be around 200K for countries however if countries such as Uganda and Ethiopia need substantially more I can try and make the case to Gates for more or for a separate funding request.

rows.reporting: Global Green Growth Institute
rows.title: Mobilising Article 6 Trading Structures (MATS)
rows.description: The objective of the Mobilising Article 6 Trading Structures (MATS) program will be to accelerate the restart of an international trading market in emission reductions, generally referred to as mitigation outcomes under the Paris Agreement. SEA have already completed a number of theoretical programs as preparation, whilst the rules of the Paris Agreement are finalized, specifically the rules related to Article 6. This program will take the next steps, developing all the lessons learned into real implementable activities with related transactable mitigation outcomes. The program is not only expected to design and ready a number of activities for transaction, but also to establish the frameworks needed for sustainable transacting in the countries selected to ensure a continued pipeline of activities can be developed. The objective will be achieved through a number of key outputs:1.       Identification and Development of Activities: potential transactable project activities will be identified, selected against set criteria and then developed against the emerging rulebook of Article 6 for international transfer.2.       Deal Structuring and Finance: GGGI will work with both Sweden as well as the host country in order to ensure as far as reasonably possible, that the two parties have established the underlying architecture needed for a successful MOPA signing and project implementation.3.       Governance Framework and Activity Approval Piloting: in order to ensure that identified project activities can proceed to transaction, with related corresponding adjustments, the appropriate governance frameworks must be in place within host countries. This output will support potential host country governments to establish such frameworks, working through the GGGI country teams and installed government contact network. Once frameworks are established, they will be tested by processing the project activities identified in Output 1.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Support to UNFPA-UNICEF Global Programme to Accelerate Action to End Child Marriage
rows.description: This project represents emergency support of $10M to contribute to the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF)/United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA) Global Programme to End Child Marriage’s COVID-19 response at the country level. Project activities include: (1) supporting the program to empower young, feminist innovators to address child marriage by partnering with innovation hubs. These hubs bring these innovators together from across Africa and South Asia to enhance their understanding of the issues around child marriage and aggregate their creative abilities to address child marriage; (2) supporting the program to launch a global communications campaign on boys and gender equality, using the lens of COVID-19 and child marriage. This campaign engages men and boys to act as champions of gender equality and to interrogate and transform harmful masculinities from an early age; and (3) supporting the program to ramp up its use of digital technologies to address child marriage by developing a coherent, uniform digital system to ensure strategic oversight, adaptability, interoperability and scale in addressing the challenges of ending child marriage in the context of COVID-19. These digital platforms facilitate the faster learning and program adaptation needed to inform Global Programme actions in the path to ending child marriage by 2030.  The project supports the UNICEF/UNFPA Global Programme to End Child Marriage, a multi-donor joint United Nations program aimed at eliminating child marriage in 12 countries in Africa and South Asia where the rates of child marriage are among the highest: Bangladesh, Burkina Faso, Ethiopia, Ghana, India, Mozambique, Nepal, Niger, Sierra Leone, Uganda, Yemen and Zambia. The project focuses on enabling girls at risk of child marriage to choose and direct their own futures, through activities aimed at the empowerment of girls, with the ultimate aim to prevent child marriage and support already married girls.  The COVID-19 pandemic is disrupting programmers aimed at ending harmful practices. UNFPA estimates this could lead, over the next decade, to 2 million additional cases of female genital mutilation/cutting and 13 million additional cases of child, early and forced marriage that would have been otherwise averted.

rows.title: Climate-smart education and green schools in Chitwan, Nepal
rows.description: Nepal is one of the most vulnerable countries to climate change, with the Global Risk Index ranking the country as the fourth most vulnerable globally. It is therefore imperative to educate Nepalis from a young age about the impacts of climate change and the various adaptation and mitigation measures associated with it. This project will focus on providing workshops to teachers on better communicating climate change in classrooms as well as in making schools greener. It will also engage with youth clubs and traditional indigenous practitioners in the region to better understand sustainable practices through inter-generation dialogues.  Additionally, the schools will serve as a learning hub for environmental and climate action for the surrounding community.

rows.description: Carry out a series on science technology, engineering and innovation seminars involving higher education institutes in India, Sri Lanka, Thailand and the UAE to reduce the pollution with macro-, micro-, and nano-plastics of urban, riverine, coastal and oceanic ecosystems and develop simple sets of policy recommendations, with a strong SS element (S Asia, NE Africa, the Gulf, SE Asia).

rows.reporting: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO)
rows.title: LINKS: Preservation and promotion of intangible cultural heritage through the recognition of  indigenous communities' knowledge, language and culture
rows.description: The project shall be leveraged to encourage the government to recognize existing traditional cultural practices of the indigenous communities in Nepal. This project shall engage youth and indigenous knowledge holders through inter-generational knowledge transmission thereby encouraging youth to continue these practices for generation. This initiative will support the translation of these practices into policies. Through this project the local communities will be encouraged to conserve their local biodiversity in the area. Indigenous knowledge is generally orally transmitted and not documented in written or recorded forms in Nepal, and hence, their knowledge is embedded within their languages. This project shall therefore, also support the promotion of the International Decade of Indigenous Languages (IDIL).

rows.reporting: Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Netherlands
rows.title: Dignified and Just lives for Dalit Women
rows.description: Nepal has made significant progress in terms of achieving targets set by MDGs. However it is failing to reduce the widening disparities between Dalit women and women from other communities. Dalit Women are the most marginalized groups in Nepal. The project addresses this societal issue in one part of the country in particular, the far west. The proposed activities aimed for improving lobbying and advocacy capacity of Dalit women through awareness raising, capacity building, advocacy and support. The project objective is to create or change policies, laws, regulations, distribution of resource or other decisions that affect Dalit women's lives and to ensure that such decisions lead to implementation.

rows.reporting: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO)
rows.title: Regional Youth Initiatives for Mental Health, Policy Advocacy, and Climate Resil
rows.description:
This multifaceted workplan aims to address critical global and regional challenges facing youth by integrating three interconnected initiatives.  

1. Global Outlook on Youth Mental Health: The UNESCO Youth Global Outlook on Youth Mental Health in Times of Crises investigates the long-term impacts of crises, such as COVID-19, on youth mental health worldwide. The initiative focuses on identifying socio-economic determinants, sources of distress, and coping mechanisms, while advocating for effective support systems. A South Asian regional consultation will ensure representation of voices from this region through focus group discussions with youth and experts, contributing qualitative insights to the global framework.  

2. Policy Advocacy in Bangladesh: In collaboration with UNESCO's Dhaka Office, this workplan supports the establishment of a nationwide mechanism in Bangladesh to amplify youth voices in policymaking. Requested by the interim government, this mechanism aims to ensure that young people's perspectives inform decision-making processes, fostering policies that genuinely address their needs and aspirations.  

3. Youth-Led Climate Resilience in Nepal: Supporting the “Climate Change Resilience and Inclusive Futures” project in Nepal, this initiative empowers youth, particularly those with disabilities, to lead evidence-based research on climate impacts and advocate for inclusive solutions. Activities include data collection, awareness campaigns, and capacity-building programs, culminating in training for young leaders to foster disability-inclusive climate advocacy. This pilot project aligns with UNESCO's priorities on education for sustainable development (ESD), disaster risk reduction, and youth inclusion, with potential for scaling across other regions.

Together, these initiatives combine research, advocacy, and capacity-building to address youth mental health, amplify youth voices in governance, and promote inclusive climate resilience, advancing UNESCO’s mission to create a sustainable and equitable future for young people worldwide.

rows.reporting: Global Green Growth Institute
rows.title: Sustainable Electric Transport in Pokhara-Bharatpur Highway
rows.description: Phase I of this project has already been approved by MT for core funding of USD 56,174 for 2023.The overall goal of this project is to solidify, extend, and strengthen implementation of low emission transport systems in intracity route within Pokhara Metropolitan City (PMC) and Bharatpur Metropolitan City (BMC), as well as intercity connecting the two metropolitan cities to ensure strong impact results and resource mobilization opportunities. The total project investment is estimated at USD 30 million assuming deployment of 130 electric buses (at approx. USD 200,000 per bus), i.e., a 10% replacement of diesel buses in operation currently in Pokhara and Bharatpur. The planned feasibility study will produce more accurate cost estimates. Also, USD 4 million will be required for technical assistance to support capacity building of all relevant stakeholders, conducting M&E, liaising with local governments for policy development, liaising with private transport operators for route aggregation, if required, etc.

rows.reporting: Global Green Growth Institute
rows.title: Green Sanitation Services Program, Phase I
rows.description: 1) To develop an enabling environment to attract finance into the sanitation sector through policy interventions and commitment from government to engage in these policy interventions. 2) To embed sanitation in the national planning framework of limate change. Through embedding sanitation in broader frameworks such as around climate resilience, GGGI will capitalize on opportunities to access climate finance and private sector financing. 3) To embed the concept of green growth into the local level sanitation planning by supporting two municipalities, where sanitation investments will be established, to develop Green Citywide Inclusive Sanitation Strategy.4) To develop innovative business models for sustainable sanitation service models in select cities of Nepal, which will be designed to be economically viable, socially acceptable and, technically and institutionally appropriate with a focus on the "sanitation value chain" which includes collection, emptying, transport, treatment and reuse/disposal5) To develop a mainstreaming strategy to replicate and scale up CWIS in other cities of Nepal and Senegal, and later in other countries using lessons learnt from this investment.

rows.reporting: Global Green Growth Institute
rows.title: KOICA MDCP 2022 - Building Climate Resilience and Reintegrating Economically Displaced Workers through Climate Smart Agriculture in the Terai Flood Plain, Nepal
rows.description: A project to build agricultural resilience and community livelihoos in the terai, one of Nepal's hardest hit and most climate vulnerable regions.The region suffers from frequent and extreme floods and draughts, which are worsening due to climate change. This region is also the hardest hit by COVID-19, generating massive health and humanitarian challenges.1) Building community and local government emergency management capacity and systems2) Building resilient agriculture and livelihoods/employment development plans, investment planning and demonstration projects for resilient agriculture value chains3) Operationalizing a challenge fund and enterprise development for resilient agriculture and livelihoods

rows.reporting: Global Green Growth Institute
rows.title: Implementation of the Nationally Determined Contribution of Nepal
rows.description: Growth of transport sector and motorization – Demand for vehicles has grown by 14% per year during 1990-2015, with much of this concentrated in and around the Kathmandu Valley region. Sustained levels of population growth in Kathmandu, and the increasing prominence of the city’s economy mean that demand for mobility in the capital will remain high for the foreseeable future. Since the 2000s, air pollution in Nepali cities, particularly Kathmandu, has worsened considerably. Levels of particulate matter in the air around Kathmandu are now similar to those of Delhi and Beijing. A 2007 study found that air pollution lead to approximately 17,000 premature deaths in Kathmandu.

rows.title: Mobilizing Resources for Bamboo Sector Acceleration
rows.description: The Nepal country office, in collaboration with INBAR, and with support from the GON, will jointly develop funding proposals to boost development of the bamboo sector. This will involve a preliminary review of funding opportunities and then the development of at least 1 funding proposal.

rows.description: In partnership with GIZ Nepal, GGGI Nepal will develop a financial mechanism (incremental cost subsidy) to support uptake of electric minibuses into public transport systems in Nepal. rows.reporting: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO)
rows.title: Safeguarding Living Heritage for the sustainable development of communities in Nepal
rows.description: The activity aims to protect the Living Heritage in Nepal by strengthening national capacities and providing policy guidance. In collaboration with UNESCO Category II Centres like ICHCAP and CRIHAP, as well as other sectors, the activity will support national capacity-building for safeguarding and promoting Living Heritage to empower communities. These efforts will focus on the role of ICH and its safeguarding in the context of climate change and integrate the safeguarding of living heritage into broader sectoral strategies for achieving sustainable development. Additionally, technical assistance will be provided to support Nepal's participation in regional periodic reporting. Nepal is among 42 countries in Asia and the Pacific undertaking its first regional cycle of reporting under the revised 2018 Overall Results Framework, 21 of which, including Nepal are submitting their reports for the first time.

rows.description:
Strengthening capacities of member states and civil societies for mapping, monitoring, and measuring cultural and creative sectors, notably in India, Sri Lanka, and Bhutan, to promote national cultural policies.

Towards better governance of creative sector and raising awareness of the 2005 Convention/its Monitoring Framework and UNESCO 1980 Recommendation concerning Status of Artists

rows.reporting: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO)
rows.title: Fostering creative sector and promoting cultural expressions in Nepal
rows.description: The activity will provide technical support towards the ratification of the 2005 Convention for the protection and promotion of cultural expressions in Nepal. Building on the experience of the recent designation of Kathmandu as UNESCO Creative City of Film, the activity will expand strategic partnership with other cities and urban centres outside Kathmandu for the promotion of UCCN and creative economy in Nepal. Through mapping, the key sectors under the Cultural and Creative Industry will be identified and consultations between Ministries and private sector will be enabled to promote the development of the sector in Nepal.

rows.title: Contribution of Culture Sector in Achieving 2030 agenda
rows.description: Strengthening the capacities of member states under the UNESCO Regional Office to promote, monitor, and measure the contribution of culture to the implementation of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, notably in the collection and analysis of data from the culture sector.

rows.reporting: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO)
rows.title: Promoting freedom of expression, access to  information
rows.description: UNESCO will work towards sensitizing and support to the member states to enhance norms and policies related to freedom of expression, press freedom, safety of journalists, including women journalists, and the right to access information, online and offline. UNESCO will organize trainings to the wide range of stakeholders - the government, journalists, media professionals and human rights actors prioritizing themes including safety of journalists, professional journalism, human rights-based approach journalism, media ethics, press freedom, data journalism, gender mainstreaming, minority-sensitive reporting, election reporting, and reporting on climate change, DRR and other crises and provide relevant expertise to support Nepali journalism and leverage that to move forward the SDGs. Media and Information Literacy will also be priority under the regular programming.

rows.reporting: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO)
rows.title: Inclusive AI and digital transformation based on human rights
rows.description: The workplan will strengthen capacity-building/processes in South Asia of governments and regulators in charge of AI/ICT policies and digital transformations strategies as well as relevant civil society organizations. The workplan will support regional and national multi-stakeholder consultations, address the gender divide in national digital transformation frameworks and  raise awareness on UNESCO's approaches to the ethics of AI  based on human rights.
